chapters.title: The Deep Kingdom
chapters.text:
Far out in the ocean, where the water is as blue as the prettiest cornflower and as clear as crystal, it is very, very deep — so deep that no cable could fathom it. There, in the deepest place of all, stands the castle of the Sea King, with walls of coral, tall windows of clearest amber, and a roof of shells that open and shut with the passing tide.
The Sea King had been a widower many years, and his old mother kept his house — a wise woman, and proud, who wore twelve oysters on her tail to mark her rank. She cared above all for her grandchildren, the six little sea-princesses; and the youngest was the loveliest of them all, with skin as clear as a rose-leaf and eyes as blue as the deepest sea.
The youngest was a strange child, quiet and thoughtful. While her sisters delighted in the treasures they gathered from the wrecks of ships, she cared for nothing but her flowers, red as the sun — and for one beautiful marble statue of a handsome boy, sunk from some lost vessel, beside which she planted a weeping rose-red willow.
The Little Mermaid: “Grandmother, tell me again of the world above the sea — of the ships and the towns, and the flowers that have fragrance, and the little birds that sing among the green trees.”
The Old Sea-Queen: “When you have reached your fifteenth year, little one, you shall rise up to the surface yourself, and sit upon the rocks in the moonlight, and watch the great ships sailing by.”
Each of the sisters was a year older than the next, so the youngest had the longest to wait — five whole years. Many a night she stood at the open window, looking up through the dark blue water at the fish, and the faint far moon, longing for the day her turn would come.

chapters.title: What the Sisters Saw
chapters.text:
One by one, as each turned fifteen, the sisters were allowed to rise. Each came back full of wonders. The eldest had lain on a sandbank near a great town and watched its thousand lights, and heard the church bells and the noise of carriages; and after her the others told of golden sunsets, of green riverbanks, and of icebergs like floating pearls, larger and loftier than any church.
The Eldest Sister: “Oh, little sister, if you could only see it — the town by the shore, and the bells, and the children laughing in the shallow water! There is nothing so beautiful in all the sea.”
The Little Mermaid: “I know that I shall love the world up there, and all the people who live in it. If only I were fifteen!”
When the five rose arm-in-arm and sang in a long row before a coming storm, the youngest would stand quite alone below, looking up after them, ready to weep — only mermaids have no tears, and so they suffer the more.

chapters.title: Her Turn at Last
chapters.text:
At last the youngest reached her fifteenth year. 'Now you are grown up,' said the old sea-queen, and she set a wreath of white lilies in her hair, each leaf half a pearl, and clasped eight great oysters to her tail to show her high rank.
The Little Mermaid: “But they hurt me so, grandmother.”
The Old Sea-Queen: “Pride must suffer pain, child. Now go — and remember all that you see.”
Gladly she would have shaken off all that grandeur; but she said her farewell and rose, as lightly as a bubble, up through the water. The sun had just set as she lifted her head above the waves, and the clouds were tinted crimson and gold, and the evening star shone soft and clear.

chapters.title: The Prince's Ship
chapters.text:
Close by lay a great ship with three masts, becalmed upon the still water. There was music and singing on board, and as darkness fell a hundred coloured lanterns were lit. The little mermaid swam up to the cabin windows, and each time the swell lifted her she could see into a hall of well-dressed people.
Among them was a young prince, the handsomest of all, with great dark eyes; it was his sixteenth birthday, and it was kept with much rejoicing. When he stepped out on deck, a hundred rockets rose hissing into the air, so bright that the little mermaid dived in fright — and when she looked up again it seemed as if all the stars of heaven were falling into the sea around her.
It grew very late, yet she could not take her eyes from the ship, or from the beautiful prince. The lanterns were put out, the rockets ceased, the cannon fell silent — but the sea had begun to moan and heave, and far off the lightning flickered. A dreadful storm was coming on.

chapters.title: The Storm
chapters.text:
The waves rose like mountains, and the great ship dived and rose among them like a swan — until at last the timbers groaned, the mainmast snapped like a reed, and the ship lay over on her side while the water rushed in. To the little mermaid it was fine sport, until she remembered that human beings cannot live in the water.
She saw the prince sink down among the dark waves. For a moment she was glad, for now he would come to her — and then she knew that he must die, and that he must not. So she swam through the drifting beams, careless that they might crush her, and dived deep, and rose, until she reached him and held his beautiful head above the water.
All through the wild night she let the waves carry them where they would, keeping his head above the sea; and toward morning the storm died, and the sun rose red and warm, but the prince's eyes stayed closed. She kissed his high, smooth forehead, and stroked back his wet hair, and thought he was like the marble boy in her garden.

chapters.title: The Sunlit Shore
chapters.text:
At last they came in sight of land, of blue mountains and green woods, and a great white building among orange trees and palms. In a still, deep little bay she bore the prince to the fine white sand and laid him in the warm sun, taking care to raise his head; then she hid herself among the rocks, and covered her face with foam, and watched.
Bells sounded in the white building, and young girls came into the garden. One of them found the prince, and seemed frightened for a moment, then fetched help; and the little mermaid saw him wake, and smile at those around him. But to her he sent no smile — he did not know that she had saved him.
The Little Mermaid: “He will never know that it was I. I carried him over the sea, and watched until the people came — and now he smiles at her, and I must go down alone into the water.”
Sorrowfully she dived down into the water and returned to her father's castle. She had always been quiet; now she grew quieter still, and would tell her sisters nothing of what she had seen.

chapters.title: The Longing for a Soul
chapters.text:
Now nothing gave her comfort but to sit in her little garden, with her arms flung round the marble boy who was so like the prince. She let her flowers grow wild, and at last she could bear her silence no longer, and asked her grandmother about the world of men.
The Little Mermaid: “If human beings are not drowned, do they live forever? Or do they die, as we do here in the sea?”
The Old Sea-Queen: “They die too, and their lives are shorter than ours. We live three hundred years, but when we end we become foam on the water, and have no grave, and no immortal soul. Human beings have a soul that lives on forever, after the body has turned to dust.”
The Little Mermaid: “I would give all my three hundred years to be a human being for a single day, and to share afterward in that heavenly world.”
The Old Sea-Queen: “There is only one way. If a man should love you more than his father and mother, and cling to you with all his heart, and a priest join your hands to be man and wife — then his soul would flow into you, and you would share in the happiness of mankind. But it can never be. The very thing that is beautiful here, your tail, they think ugly on the earth.”
The Little Mermaid: “Then I will go to the sea witch, of whom I have always been so afraid. She can give me counsel and help.”
That evening there was a court ball, splendid beyond anything on earth, and the little mermaid sang more sweetly than any there. But her heart was above the water, and while the palace was all gladness and song, she stole away alone, and took the road she had never dared to take.

chapters.title: The Road to the Witch
chapters.text:
Behind the roaring whirlpools lay the sorceress's country. The little mermaid had to cross where the water spun like foaming mill-wheels, and then a long stretch of warm bubbling mire the witch called her turf-moor. Beyond it stood a wood where every tree and bush was a polyp, half animal and half plant, with long slimy arms and fingers like worms.
In their grasp she saw the white bones of the drowned, oars and rudders and chests — and one little mermaid they had caught and strangled. Her heart beat with terror; but she thought of the prince and of the soul she longed for, bound her long hair tight about her head, folded her arms, and darted through like a fish, between the writhing fingers, to the house beyond, built of the bones of shipwrecked men.

chapters.title: The Bargain
chapters.text:
The Sea Witch: “I know what you want. You wish to be rid of your fish's tail, and have two clumsy props instead, so the young prince may love you and you may win a soul. It is very stupid of you — but you shall have your way.”
The Little Mermaid: “I will bear it, whatever the pain.”
The Sea Witch: “Every step you take will be as though you trod on sharp knives, until the blood must flow. And if the prince weds another, the first morning after, your heart will break, and you will become foam upon the sea. Will you still have it?”
The Sea Witch: “But I must be paid, and it is no trifle I ask. You have the sweetest voice of any in the sea, and with it you think to charm him. That voice you must give to me — the best thing you possess, for the price of my draught.”
The Little Mermaid: “But if you take my voice, what is left to me?”
The Sea Witch: “Your lovely form, your gliding walk, and your speaking eyes. With those, surely, you can win a heart. Come — put out your little tongue, that I may cut it off in payment, and the draught is yours.”
The witch set her cauldron on the fire and scoured it with living snakes; she pricked her breast and let the black blood drip, and the steam rose in shapes too dreadful to look upon. When the draught was done it was clear as the purest water. Then she cut out the little mermaid's tongue, so that she was dumb, and could never speak or sing again.
Draught in hand, she passed back through the wood — and the polypi shrank away in terror from the thing that shone in her grasp like a twinkling star. She saw her father's palace dark and sleeping, kissed her hand a thousand times toward it, and rose up through the deep blue water, dumb, and leaving her home forever.

chapters.title: Feet Like Knives
chapters.text:
Before the prince's marble steps, in the clear moonlight, she drank the draught. It was as if a two-edged sword went through her; she swooned, and lay like one dead. When the sun rose over the sea she woke to a sharp pain — and there before her stood the handsome prince. Her tail was gone; she had two pretty white legs, and no dancer ever trod so lightly. But at every step it was as though she walked on knives, and she could not speak a word.
The Prince: “Who are you, and where have you come from? You look at me so gently, and yet you say nothing at all.”
She could only look at him with her deep blue eyes. He took her into the palace and dressed her in silk, and she became the loveliest creature there — and the most silent. She was given leave to sleep at his door on a velvet cushion, and rode with him through the woods and up the mountains, and though her tender feet bled, she only smiled and followed him. He called her his little foundling, and loved her as one loves a dear child.
The Prince: “You are dearer to me than any, with those speaking eyes. You are so like a maiden I once saw — she found me on the shore when I was wrecked, and saved my life. She belongs to a holy temple, and I shall never meet her again; but you are like her, and good fortune has sent you to me instead.”

chapters.title: The Bride
chapters.text:
Soon it was said the prince must marry the beautiful daughter of a neighbouring king, and a fine ship was fitted out. He said he went only to pay a visit; but a great company sailed with him, and the little mermaid among them, and she knew his thoughts better than any.
But when the princess came, the little mermaid saw that it was the very maiden of the temple, who had found the prince upon the shore. His face lit with a joy she had never seen, for he believed that this was the girl who had saved his life.
The Prince: “It was you — you who saved my life when I lay as dead upon the beach! My dearest hopes are all fulfilled. Rejoice for me, my silent one, for you love me best of all.”
The Little Mermaid: “Ah, he does not know that it was I. I would give up my life for his sake, and he will never know.”
The bells rang, and the bride and bridegroom joined hands and received the bishop's blessing; and the little mermaid, dressed in silk and gold, held up the bride's train. But her ears heard nothing of the music, and her eyes saw nothing of the holy rite, for she thought only of the night of death that was coming, and of all she had lost in the world.

chapters.title: Foam and the Daughters of the Air
chapters.text:
On the wedding ship the lamps were lit and the sailors danced, and the little mermaid danced too, though her feet were as knives, for this was the last evening she would ever see the prince. Long after midnight, when the bride and bridegroom slept in their purple tent, she leaned on the vessel's edge and watched the east for the first grey light — the dawn that would end her.
The Eldest Sister: “We have given our long hair to the witch, that she might help you, so you need not die tonight. She has given us a knife — here! Before the sun rises you must plunge it into the prince's heart; when his warm blood falls on your feet they will grow again into a tail, and you will come back to us.”
She drew back the crimson curtain and saw the fair bride sleeping with her head on the prince's breast. She bent and kissed his brow, and looked once at the sharpening dawn, and once at the knife — and flung it far out into the waves, where the water reddened as if with drops of blood.
Then she cast herself from the ship into the sea, and felt her body dissolving into foam. But she did not die. The sun rose warm upon the water, and all around her floated hundreds of transparent, lovely beings, through whom she could see the white sails and the red morning clouds.
The Little Mermaid: “Where am I?”
A Daughter of the Air: “Among the daughters of the air. A mermaid has no immortal soul, and can win one only through a human's love — but we, by our own good deeds, may earn a soul ourselves. You have suffered and endured with your whole heart, and raised yourself to us; and in three hundred years of kindness you too shall win a soul.”
She lifted her clear eyes toward the sun, and for the first time in her life she felt them fill with tears. On the ship below, the prince and his bride were searching for her, gazing sorrowfully at the pearly foam. Unseen, she kissed the bride's forehead and fanned the prince, and rose with the children of the air upon a rosy cloud, up into the wide and shining sky.

chapters.title: A Soul of Her Own
chapters.text: She did not die. Where the foam should have taken her she rose instead among the daughters of the air, and felt the sun warm upon a body of her own. She had loved with her whole heart, given up voice and home and life itself, and asked for nothing back — and that, the air-children told her, had already begun the work of three hundred years. Unseen, she kissed the bride's forehead and fanned the sleeping prince, and mounted on a rosy cloud into the wide bright sky. For every good and gentle child she should find in the world below, a year of her waiting would fall away; and so, sooner than she knew, she would come at last into a soul, and a heaven, and a joy that does not end.
